stories.title: COS Wildfire Preparedness: Fire Weather Watch + CSFD Ready, Set, Go
stories.angle: The "24 Fire" is 86% contained but a Fire Weather Watch is now active for March 30 in El Paso County (gusts to 40 mph, humidity as low as 10%). CSFD launched the "Ready, Set, Go" wildfire preparedness campaign March 11. Burn Restriction Order 2026-R1 is in effect. Parents need to sign up for Peak Alerts (PeakAlerts.org) now — before the next fire event.

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text: Fire Weather Watch for El Paso County Monday. Gusts to 40 mph, humidity as low as 10%. The "24 Fire" is 86% contained but conditions are still dangerous. Burning prohibited. Sign up: PeakAlerts.org 🔥 #COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text: CSFD Ready, Set, Go! tip: sign up for Peak Alerts using your HOME, WORK, and kids' SCHOOL addresses — not just home. Official alerts only: PeakAlerts.org (not third-party apps). Info: ColoradoSprings.gov/ReadySetGo #COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

facebook.long_form:
```
Colorado Springs families — this is your reminder to sign up for Peak Alerts before Monday's fire weather arrives.

A Fire Weather Watch is in effect for El Paso County on Monday, March 30. Conditions: west winds 15-25 mph with gusts up to 40 mph, and relative humidity dropping as low as 10%. Those are the kinds of numbers that let small fires grow into major emergencies fast. The City's Burn Restriction Order 2026-R1 is in effect — no outdoor burning of any kind.

The "24 Fire" southwest of Colorado Springs (near Highway 115) has reached 86% containment as of this week. That's good news. But fire season isn't over, and conditions like Monday's are exactly why preparedness matters year-round.

The Colorado Springs Fire Department launched its "Ready, Set, Go!" wildfire preparedness campaign earlier this month. The key action item for every COS family: sign up for Peak Alerts at PeakAlerts.org. Use your home address, your work address, AND your children's school addresses so you receive alerts for all locations that matter to your family. Use only official channels — not third-party apps.

Full resources are at ColoradoSprings.gov/ReadySetGo.

Have you signed your family up for Peak Alerts yet?
```
facebook.image_caption:
#### Image Caption -- 10:00 AM MT

```
Fire Weather Watch active Monday. Sign up for Peak Alerts NOW. PeakAlerts.org — home, work, and school addresses.
```

stories.title: CPSC Recalls March 2026: HALO Dream Sleepsuit + More
stories.angle: Three new CPSC recalls relevant to families with young children: the HALO Dream sleepsuit (45,000 units, burn hazard, March 5), Bellabu Bear children's robes (burn hazard), and GLMZZ Fidget Magnet Ball Toys (intestinal perforation hazard). A roundup format helps parents check multiple products at once.

x_posts.label: Post 2
x_posts.text: Three CPSC recalls: HALO Dream Magic Sleepsuits (45K units, choking hazard — zipper head detaches), Bellabu Bear robes (burn hazard), GLMZZ magnets (intestinal hazard — stop use now). Check cpsc.gov/Recalls 🚨 #COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

facebook.long_form:
```
Three CPSC recalls this month that COS families with young children should check.

First: HALO Dream Magic Sleepsuits. About 45,000 units were recalled on March 5. The zipper head can detach from the sleepsuit, posing a choking hazard to infants. These were sold at Halosleep.com, Amazon, Walmart, and Target from September 2025 through February 2026 for around $50. HALO is one of the most widely used infant sleepwear brands in the country, so there's a good chance some of you have one of these. Check the CPSC website for affected model numbers.

Second: Bellabu Bear Children's Robes. Also recalled for violating the flammability standard for children's sleepwear. These were sold at Saks Fifth Avenue and boutiques from January 2024 through July 2025 for around $60. If you bought one as a gift or picked one up at a boutique, it's worth checking.

Third: GLMZZ Fidget Magnet Ball Toys. This one is the most urgent. High-powered magnets, when swallowed, attract each other inside the body through intestinal walls and can cause perforation, blood poisoning, and death. If your child has these toys, stop use immediately and contact GLMZZrecall@outlook.com for a full refund.

Full recall details are at cpsc.gov/Recalls.

Do you check the CPSC recalls page regularly? A lot of parents don't know it exists — share this post so others can check too.
```
facebook.image_caption:
#### Image Caption -- 10:00 AM MT

```
Three CPSC recalls this month: HALO Dream Magic Sleepsuits (choking hazard), Bellabu Bear robes (burn hazard), GLMZZ magnet toys (intestinal hazard). Check cpsc.gov/Recalls.
```

stories.title: AAP 2026 Immunization Schedule vs. CDC Vaccine Controversy
stories.angle: The AAP just released the 2026 Recommended Childhood Immunization Schedule, now formally separate from the federal CDC schedule. The AAP has publicly called the CDC's plan to reclassify childhood vaccines "dangerous and unnecessary." Key updates: clesrovimab added for RSV in infants, HPV recommended starting at age 9-12, COVID-19 universally recommended for 6-23 months. COS families should talk to their pediatrician.

x_posts.label: Post 3
x_posts.text: AAP released the 2026 childhood vaccine schedule — now separate from the CDC's. New: RSV option for newborns, HPV at age 9. AAP called the CDC's tiered vaccine plan "dangerous and unnecessary." Ask your pediatrician. #COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

facebook.long_form:
```
Something significant happened in the world of childhood vaccines this month, and it's worth knowing about before your next well-child visit.

The American Academy of Pediatrics released its 2026 Recommended Childhood and Adolescent Immunization Schedule — and for the first time, it's formally separate from the federal CDC schedule. The AAP schedule is endorsed by 12 major medical organizations including the AMA and ACOG.

Key updates in the 2026 AAP schedule: clesrovimab, a new RSV prevention option for infants (with guidance that depends on whether the mother received an RSV vaccine during pregnancy); HPV vaccination recommended starting at ages 9-12; and COVID-19 vaccination universally recommended for children ages 6-23 months.

Why the separation? The CDC announced plans to reclassify childhood vaccines into tiers — "recommended for all," "recommended for high-risk children only," and "shared clinical decision-making" — a significant departure from how the vaccine schedule has always worked. The AAP publicly called this change "dangerous and unnecessary" and said it upends the longstanding expert review process.

For COS families, the most important thing right now is to ask your pediatrician which schedule their practice follows. Most pediatric practices in Colorado follow the AAP's recommendations.

What questions do you have about the 2026 vaccine schedule? Drop them below.
```
facebook.image_caption:
#### Image Caption -- 10:00 AM MT

```
AAP's 2026 childhood vaccine schedule is now separate from the CDC's. Ask your pediatrician which one they follow.
```

stories.title: COS Parks 2026: What's Coming for Families This Spring
stories.angle: Colorado Springs Parks, Recreation and Cultural Services (PRCS) is sharing 2026 updates for families. A new 10-15-year Park System Master Plan is in progress with community input closing this spring. Stratton Neighborhood Park is in community planning with a 2026 opening goal. Starsmore Visitor Center (Cheyenne Canyon) is getting accessibility upgrades this year.

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text: COS parks 2026: Stratton Neighborhood Park targets opening this year. Starsmore Visitor Center gets accessibility upgrades. New Park Master Plan — community input open now. Parks open; burning prohibited. #COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

facebook.long_form:
```
COS outdoor spaces are open this spring — and there's a lot happening to improve them for families.

A few updates from Colorado Springs Parks, Recreation and Cultural Services (PRCS):

The 2026 Park System Master Plan is underway. It's the city's 10-to-15-year guide for parks, trails, open spaces, community centers, and rec programs — and it's in the community input phase right now. If you want a say in what COS parks look like for the next decade, visit coloradosprings.gov/ParkSystemMasterPlan.

Stratton Neighborhood Park is in active planning in 2026, with a goal of opening the site to the public this year. If you're in that part of COS, watch for updates.

Starsmore Visitor Center in Cheyenne Canyon is getting accessibility upgrades and expanded programming space this year. It's one of the most family-friendly natural attractions in COS — those improvements are welcome.

One reminder while you're out there: Burn Restriction Order 2026-R1 is in effect for Colorado Springs. That means no outdoor burning, including campfires. Parks are fully open for hiking, wildlife watching, and outdoor recreation — just no fires.

What's your family's favorite COS outdoor spot? We're always looking for new recommendations.
```
facebook.image_caption:
#### Image Caption -- 10:00 AM MT

```
COS parks 2026: Stratton opening this year, Starsmore upgrades, new Master Plan with community input now open. Parks are open — outdoor burning is prohibited.
```

stories.title: Affordable Childcare in COS: Zach's Place at Special Kids Special Families
stories.angle: With full-time daycare in Colorado Springs averaging nearly $20/hour, options for families on tight budgets can feel limited. Special Kids Special Families' Zach's Place at the Laurie Hillyard Family Center operates on a sliding-fee scale and welcomes children with and without disabilities in inclusive classrooms — a rare combination in COS.

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text: COS full-time daycare is averaging close to $20/hour. Zach's Place at Special Kids Special Families uses a sliding-fee scale and serves kids with and without disabilities together. More info: sksfcolorado.org #COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

facebook.long_form:
```
If you're feeling the crunch of COS childcare costs, you're not imagining it.

Full-time daycare in Colorado Springs is averaging close to $20 an hour — one of the higher rates in the state. For families without flexible workplace arrangements or a nearby grandparent, the math can get punishing fast.

One option worth knowing about: Zach's Place at the Laurie Hillyard Family Center, operated by Special Kids Special Families. It uses a sliding-fee-scale model, which means what you pay is based on your household income — not a fixed market rate. It also serves children with and without disabilities in the same inclusive classrooms, which is a rarity in the local childcare landscape.

Inclusive childcare has well-documented developmental benefits for all children involved, not just those with disabilities — stronger social skills, better peer modeling, and broader exposure to different ways of communicating and navigating the world.

If you're looking for more information or want to see if Zach's Place is a fit for your family, visit sksfcolorado.org.

What other affordable childcare options have you found in COS? Share in the comments — other parents can always use the tip.
```
facebook.image_caption:
#### Image Caption -- 10:00 AM MT

```
COS daycare averaging ~$20/hour. Zach's Place at Special Kids Special Families: sliding-fee scale, inclusive classrooms. sksfcolorado.org
```
